narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the university's machine learning model predicted a 64.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your admission application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your low class rank in Year 1 and Year 3—both placing you in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—your notably low LSAT score well below the average, a middle family income bracket that's about average, an undergraduate GPA of 3.50 that's actually stronger than most applicants, being female in a slightly male-dominated pool, Asian background which is less common than White applicants, and your plan to attend full-time like the vast majority. Among these, your Year 3 class decile had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward predicting failure, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, your LSAT score, family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender in that order of impact. Each of these factors nudged the prediction slightly more toward failure rather than success, with your low school deciles and LSAT standing out as the heaviest weights because they deviated so much from what the model sees in students who typically pass. Your solid undergraduate GPA tried to help by suggesting potential, but it wasn't enough to offset the others in the model's view. Compared to typical students who pass, you're in a tougher spot with those bottom-decile school rankings and LSAT, though your GPA gives you a brighter edge than average. It's tough to hear, especially since elements like gender, race, and full-time plans are what they are and hard to change. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost your chances by focusing on improving your academic performance in law school to climb those class deciles—perhaps through better study habits or tutoring—since that's the top factor. Retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the average score around 37 could make a big difference too, as it's a key influencer and often improvable with preparation. Strengthening any family support or financial stability might help marginally, but prioritizing grades and test prep will likely have the biggest payoff for future applications or bar success. You're already ahead with that strong undergrad GPA, so build on it—many students turn things around with targeted effort. Keep pushing; this isn't the end of your path to law.
